rulings.Case Title: BRIAN MCVEIGH VS. SF RECYCLING & DISPOSAL, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Response And Production Plaintiffs Post-Trial Document Request Set No. One
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, July 22, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF BRIAN MCVEIGH'S Motion To Compel Further Response And Production Plaintiffs Post-Trial Document Request Set No. One. Pro Tem Judge Noah Lebowitz, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Parties to appear. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to noah@ndllegal.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: EMILY ARREOLA ET AL VS. DAHVIE JAMES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Dahvie James, Philip Watt, Field & Pond, Llc Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, July 22, 2019, Line 2, Defendants Dahvie James, Philip Watt, Field & Pond, LLC's Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint [Added to calendar for entry of tentative ruling only] [Part 2 of 2]. Defendants misplace their reliance on the principle that the CLRA does not apply to transactions involving the sale of real estate. (Civ. Code § 1754; McKell v. Washington Mutual, Inc. (2006) 142 Cal.App.4th 1457, 1488.) Likewise, the Court of Appeal's opinion in Heckart v. A-1 Storage, Inc. (2015) 243 Cal.App.4th 525, opn. superseded, Heckart v. A-1 Self Storage, Inc. (2018) 4 Cal.5th 749 held only that the CLRA did not apply to misrepresentations about a customer goods protection plan provided in connection with the rental of storage space, in part because the protection plan was ancillary to the main purpose of the rental agreement. Ancillary services provided by the sellers of intangible goods include "additional customer services related to the maintenance, value, use, redemption, resale, or repayment of" intangible goods not covered under the CLRA, such as investment securities, bank deposit accounts and loans. (Fairbanks, 46 Cal.4th at 65.) Here, in contrast, the goods and services were not ancillary to the parties' agreement, but were an essential element of that agreement. Further, Defendants' alleged misrepresentations went to the core of whether they could even legally supply the goods and services offered under the venue rental agreement. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Plaintiffs are required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and states that the motion was not opposed, and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. [End of tentative ruling] =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: EMILY ARREOLA ET AL VS. DAHVIE JAMES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, July 22, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANTS DAHVIE JAMES, PHILIP WATT, FIELD & POND, LLC's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. [Part 1 of 2]. Defendants' demurrer to the first cause of action of the first amended complaint is overruled. The Consumer Legal Remedies Act ("CLRA") prohibits unfair or deceptive acts undertaken "in a transaction intended to result or that results in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer." (Civ. Code § 1770.) The CLRA defines "[s]ervices" as "work, labor, and services for other than a commercial or business use, including services furnished in connection with the sale or repair of goods." (Id. § 1761(b).) It defines "goods" as "tangible chattels bought or leased for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes . . . ." (Id. § 1761(a).) Among the unlawful practices specifically prohibited by the CLRA are "[m]isrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services" (id. § 1170(a)(2)); "[r]epresenting that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that he or she does not have" (id. § 1170(a)(5); and "[r]epresenting that a transaction confers or involves rights, remedies, or obligations that it does not have or involve, or that are prohibited by law." (Id. § 1170(a)(14).) The CLRA must "be liberally construed and applied to promote its underlying purposes, which are to protect consumers against unfair and deceptive business practices and to provide efficient and economical procedures to secure such protection." (Id. § 1760.) In this case, Plaintiffs allege that Defendants fraudulently agreed to rent a wedding venue to Plaintiffs. The parties' agreement contemplated the provision of both goods (e.g., chairs, linens, plates, flatware, glasses, etc.) and services (e.g., setup and clean-up), and thus was "intended to result in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer." Applying the plain meaning of the statutory terms, Plaintiffs' allegations that Defendants reneged on the agreement therefore is sufficient to state a cause of action for violation of the CLRA. Indeed, in Fairbanks v. Superior Court (2009) 46 Cal.4th 56, in discussing the legislative history of the CLRA, the California Supreme Court noted that the Legislature adopted the Act largely from a model law, the National Consumer Act, which broadly defined "services" to include " 'privileges with respect to transportation, hotel and restaurant accommodations, education, entertainment, recreation, physical culture, hospital accommodations, funeral, cemetery accommodations, and the like . . .'" (Id. at 61(emphasis added).) [Continued to Part 2 of 2] =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GARY JARUMAY ET AL VS. LILY GROVE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative For Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, July 22, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANT LILY GROVE's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative For Summary Adjudication. Defendant Lily Grove's motion for summary judgment or alternatively, summary adjudication is denied in its entirety. Defendant fails to sustain her summary judgment burden. First, claim/issue preclusion does not apply to this case. The probate proceeding did not decide whether decedent was the victim of fraud or elder abuse. (See Estate of Dito (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 791, 802, 805 [petition asserting claim of financial elder abuse was not barred by res judicata where it raised a different primary right from that considered in the prior probate proceeding].) Further, Defendant does not address Plaintiffs' allegation in the first amended complaint, or their factual showing, that much of the conduct to which they object was unknown to them at the time of the probate proceedings because of Defendant's alleged concealment of facts, and therefore was not and could not have the subject of objections filed in that proceeding. (FAC 30, 31, 36, 37; Jarumay Decl. 8-9, 11, 12.) (See Teselle v. McLoughlin (2009) 173 Cal.App.4th 156, 173-176 [defendants' motion for summary adjudication of elder financial abuse claim was insufficient where it failed to refute a tenable pleaded theory and did not conclusively negate an element of the cause of action].) Second, Defendant's "factually devoid" argument fails as Defendant does not specifically highlight any factually devoid discovery responses in compliance with California Rule of Court 3.1350(h). Rather, Defendant generally cites to Plaintiff's discovery responses. (See Defendant's Undisputed Material Facts 15, 16, 17, 18, 21, 22, 24.) The procedural deficiencies in Defendant's moving papers render them insufficient to meet her burden. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and states that the motion was not opposed, and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN SOLOMON VS. ALFREDO HANS LOCHT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Entry Of Default And Default Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, July 22, 2019, Line 10, DEFENDANT ALFREDO LOCHT's Motion To Set Aside Entry Of Default And Default Judgment. Defendant Alfredo Hans Locht's motion to vacate the default entered on March 7, 2019 is denied. There is no default judgment in this case. Defendant's motion pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure § 473.5 fails because the default was not entered as a result of a lack of "actual notice." The record demonstrates that Defendant was properly served with the summons and complaint on November 29, 2018, and had notice of the action prior to entry of the default. (Locht Decl. 3, 6; Jawandha Decl. 4, 5 & Ex. A.) Further, he was represented by counsel, who was notified that his default would be taken, and who obtained several extensions of time on his behalf. (Jawandha Decl. 7.) Defendant fails to maintain his burden for relief pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure § 473. That provision requires that a motion for relief be brought within "a reasonable time." In this case, Defendant was aware of the default in early March 2019 and without explanation delayed over three months before filing the instant motion on June 24. (See Benjamin v. Dalmo Mfg. Co. (1948) 31 Cal.2d 523, 531 [unexplained three-month delay unreasonable]; Younessi v. Woolf (2016) 244 Cal.App.4th 1137, 1145 [unexplained seven-week delay unreasonable].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and states that the motion was not opposed, and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GARY STAPLETON VS. BURLINGAME POINT LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Transfer Venue
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, July 22, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANT HATHAWAY DINWIDDIE CONSTRUCTION COMPANY's Motion To Transfer Venue. Defendants Hathaway Dinwiddie Construction Company and Critchfield Mechanical, Inc.'s motion to change venue based on the convenience of witnesses and ends of justice pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure § 397 is denied without prejudice as premature. Such a motion may not be brought until all defendants have answered. (Code Civ. Proc. § 396b(a); Cholakian & Associates v. Superior Court (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 361, 372 ["answers of all proper defendants [must] be filed before an opposition to a motion to transfer to a correct court will be considered on the basis of convenience of witnesses or the ends of justice"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and states that the motion was not opposed, and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
